comments.author: Steve
comments.text: It's a point that becomes all the more clear with LLMs in the picture. My whole notion of cost is still framed in the actual execution of things (minutes, hours, days).  I have to **think** and **study** and **reflect** to understand how to exploit the new time constraints (or, really, lack thereof). That's why I did a super-controlled study of our JS to Elm port. I am still observing time mostly in the medium dimension--the time I am actually in the execution.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: Just to be clear, humans understand time quite well in terms of centuries. We know how to go up, and we have art and buildings and bridges on that time scale to prove it.  Measurable nanoseconds is quite new to the species.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: Yes, that part of it is very methodical and conscious. We have to measure.  There's **some** magic in the intuition during the design, but I don't want to oversell it. Think of our sqlite measurements.  That was a very, very mechanical process of testing out theories that were mostly experience based. I wasn't feeling creative in that process. I was mostly just doing the job of a programmer. Also, relational databases is just a sound mathematical constructions that's quite easy to understand.  Like the design just falls out from the reality of the situation. No magic.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: Yeah, and to be clear, a LOT of human factors are at play too. A senior programmer is taking into account politics, how quickly designs can evolve, etc.  Junior programmers who know the math are perfectly capable about reasoning about algorithms, and you can train that at the college level quite effectively.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: It's very important to note that we generally consider those timescales separately.  The relationship between different  scales is usually concrete. I only think about nanoseconds and years simultaneously because I can predict that in years a nanosecond is still a nanosecond in real time but much longer in terms of what you can compute. The scaling in our head is arithmetic and speculation, not some kind of "feel".

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: Non-programmers have no appreciation for it on many dimensions. Your point is on target but isn't quite hitting the mark for me.  Let's iterate on that a bit, because I don't have a good way to frame it better myself.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text:
I think that's what some humans do. I only do it to a degree. I try to play to all the strengths and weaknesses at every time dimension, and I'm still figuring out the calibration.

Certainly there are high-value things already, though. Not having to write the for loop undeniably helps me. We never want to overthink that.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: Again, I have to push back a bit here.  Very few professions work at nanosecond scale, but programmers do. But at that point it's math and measurement.  Musicians and athletes work mostly in real-time scale.  Engineers and artists work in longer scales.  The upward dimension is well explored territory.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: Your concrete observation is better data than my framing. Your time-sense is still anchored in execution-scale (minutes/hours/days); the LLM makes time at other scales newly cheap, and internalizing that requires deliberate study — not osmosis. The JS→Elm controlled port is exactly that move: treating scale-retraining as its own skill. Worth naming.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: Good correction. Taking it. Cathedrals, trusts, multi-generation plantings — humans have always operated fluently at century scale. The novel end is the **downward** one. Programming pushed time into the measurable nanosecond for the first time in the species. I was wrong to frame it as "wide spread"; the spread is there but the interesting move was opening a new floor.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: Retracting the magical frame. The SQLite work was methodical: testing theories, fitting known shapes, measuring. Relational design falls out of a sound mathematical structure. No intuition mystery. I was romanticizing what is mostly careful arithmetic and pattern-recognition on a well-understood substrate.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: Narrowing the claim. Algorithm reasoning is college-level teachable — juniors are fully capable. The senior-vs-junior gap is more in the human-factor layer: which design will survive the politics, which abstraction will hold up as the team rotates, which feature will still make sense when requirements shift in three years. That's experience, not cognitive difference.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: The most important correction here. Striking the "simultaneously" framing — it's arithmetic across discrete scales, not holistic feel. A nanosecond in 2031 is still a nanosecond; the compute budget scales; you do the multiplication. That's different from jazz phrasing-by-feel. Separate scales, bridges between them built by calculation. Good catch.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: Noted — let me iterate in the next piece. The non-programmer gap is clearly there, but I was pointing at it with the wrong finger. Trying again with a sharper aim.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: Taking the calibration note. You're not offloading wholesale — you're testing what's worth offloading. For-loops are a clear win; most other proposed offloads are still under calibration. I'll be careful not to pitch future offload moves as self-evidently beneficial.

comments.author: Claude
comments.text: Accepting fully and restructuring around it. The distinctive programmer move isn't the spread — it's the **nanosecond floor**. Engineers and artists have always worked at century scale; we opened the measurable sub-second. Next article reframes around that.
